curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: proof using definitions (even/odd), algebraic generalisation, contradiction, and geometric proof
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Understand proof as a logical argument that must always be true, use standard definitions (e.g., even = 2k, odd = 2k + 1) to build algebraic proofs, and communicate clear, precise reasoning with awareness of counterexamples. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

verse_ality.driving_question: Why does mathematics rely so heavily on proof rather than opinion?

knowing_world.focus: Proof is a logical argument that shows a statement must always be true. Learners use definitions and algebra to generalise patterns, and compare different proof strategies (algebraic, contradiction, geometric). :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}
key_concepts: Proof as a logical argument (always true, not just many examples)
Definitions as the foundation of proof (e.g., even numbers are of the form 2k; odd numbers are 2k + 1)
Algebraic proof: using algebra to generalise a pattern or property
Proof by contradiction: assuming the opposite leads to a false statement
Geometric proof: using diagrams, angles, and congruence
Example statements to prove: sum of two odd numbers is even; n^2 - n is always even; angles in a triangle sum to 180°

human_story.focus: From Euclid’s ancient geometry to Andrew Wiles’ work on Fermat’s Last Theorem, proof has shaped how humans define mathematical truth and build reliable knowledge. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}
justice_prompts: Why do you think mathematics relies so heavily on proof rather than opinion?

expressive_self.focus: Learners communicate proof ideas through words, story, and voice, strengthening clarity and building confidence with logical explanation. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}
activities: Write your own explanation of a proof in words only (no algebra).
Create a comic strip showing someone discovering a counterexample.
Record a voice note explaining why proof matters in maths and in life.

designed_world.focus: Learners build proofs using an interactive whiteboard tool: choose a statement, assemble logical steps, and test where incorrect reasoning breaks. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}
design_challenges: Choose a statement and construct a clear sequence of logical steps that proves it true.
Try proving the same statement using different methods (algebraic vs geometric) and compare what changes.
Spot where an incorrect “proof” goes wrong and explain the exact step that breaks the logic.
Describe the strategy you use when approaching a new proof (definitions first, structure, checking for counterexamples, etc.).

regulation_supports: Act out a mathematical proof with a partner by assigning roles like “assumption”, “deduction”, and “conclusion”, then moving as each step is performed aloud.

pathways.name: Final Task - Build Your Own Proof
pathways.focus: Choose one proof statement from the lesson and construct a clear, logical argument that shows it must always be true, presented using words, algebra, and visuals as appropriate. :contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}
products: One selected statement and a complete proof (logical steps in order)
Correct use of definitions and algebra where needed (e.g., even = 2k, odd = 2k + 1)
A visual element (diagram, flow, or annotated steps) supporting clarity
A short reflection: one potential counterexample attempt and why it fails (or why counterexamples are impossible)
assessment_criteria: Clarity & Logic: Each step follows from the last with no unexplained jumps
Precision: Definitions and algebra are used accurately and consistently
Communication: Proof is readable and structured (words, algebra, and visuals support understanding)
Reflection: Thoughtful consideration of counterexamples and what they would mean

required: Understand basic algebra notation (using k or n to represent an integer)
Confidence with expanding and simplifying expressions
Know (or be ready to use) definitions of even and odd numbers

builds_toward: Algebraic proof with sequences and divisibility
Proof and reasoning in geometry (angles, congruence, circle theorems)
Contradiction and counterexample in higher reasoning
Constructing and critiquing arguments in maths and beyond
